description: Sahyadri Hills in Maharashtra is home to one of 12 Jyotirlingas, called Bhimshankar. The temple dedicated to Lord Shiva is a major draw for the pilgrims. In addition, the splendid natural beauty and wildlife reserve around the temple attracts nature lovers and wildlife explorers. Trekking is a thrilling activity to access the temple and many nearby attractions and this attracts flocks of adventurers to the place. Bhimshankar Temple has self-manifested Lingam of Shiva who according to the legends appeared here to slay demons like Bhima and Tripurasur. The temple is built in Nagara and Indo-Aryan architectural styles and is located on the bank of River Bhima which is believed to have formed from Shiva’s sweat. Other attractions of the temple include a shrine dedicated to Shani God and a Portuguese bell. Many beautiful lakes, waterfalls and streams greet the visitors on the way to the temple. One can also visit other attractions like Gupt Bhimashankar (a naturally formed Shiva Lingam), Kamalaja Temple and Bhimashankar Wildlife Sanctuary. Warnings Trekking can be daunting for the first-timers. Be careful when trekking during monsoons.

description: Carved in the basalt rocks of Western Ghats’ Satamala Range of Hills, Pitalkhora Caves have their history dating back to the 3rd century BC. Counted among the specimen of the earliest Buddhist sites, the caves were discovered by the British in the 19th century. Representing exquisite rock-cut architecture, the cluster consists of 14 Buddhist caves out of which, 4 represent chaityas (prayer halls) and 10 representing viharas (monasteries). The stupas at the chaityas are considered among the best across the country. Among the most amazing highlights of the place are the enthralling paintings and inscriptions on the pillars. The statues and sculptures of nagas (snakes), door guardians and animals like elephants and horses are exceptionally remarkable. Miniature windows and sculptures of yakshas enhance the visual treat. A notable aspect of many caves is the drainage system which was constructed to prevent water from seeping inside. The hike to the caves in itself is an outstanding experience. The caves overlook the ravines and gorges and have beautiful waterfalls all around. Avoid visiting the site during monsoons when the entire surface becomes slippery.

description: Named after an ancient temple, Nandur Madhyameshwar Bird Sanctuary has recently earned acclaim as an important bird area. The sanctuary which resulted from the construction of a dam on River Godavari spreads over a vast protected area of about 10,012 hectare. Over the course of time, multiple lakes and shallow water ponds formed in the region led to the perfect habitats for numerous species of native and migratory birds. Among the top species of residential birds found here are black ibis, egrets, herons, vultures, partridges, teals, sandpipe, storks, kites, buzzards, spotbills and many more. The commonly spotted migratory birds at the sanctuary include spoonbills, pintails, pochardds, goode brahminy duck, curlews, flamingo, godwits and many others. The sanctuary also features a huge variety of flora, including trees like mango, jamun, tamarind, neem, maharukh and babul and many aquatic plants as well. Visitors may also encounter the presence of animals like tigers, leopard, panther, bear, bison, blue bull, palm civet and mongoose. Boating is also possible at one of the lakes in the sanctuary. There are many ancient temples in the close vicinity.

description: Known for its resemblance with Agra’s Taj Mahal, Bibi ka Maqbara is a beautiful mausoleum and a popular tourist spot in Maharashtra. Interestingly, Taj Mahal was commissioned by Shah Jahan in the memory of his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal and Bibi ka Maqbara was commissioned by his grandson Azam Shah for his mother Dilras Banu Begum. Another interesting aspect is that this monument was designed by Ata-Ullah, whose father Ustad Ahmad Lahauri was the chief designer of Taj Mahal. Though not as splendid as Taj Mahal, this mausoleum is admirable for its architectural elements like impressive foliage designs, carved motifs, intricate details, marble dome and woodwork. Other highlights of the monument include lush green lawns, rows of beautiful trees, pools and fountains, a mosque and a serene environment. Popularly, it is known as Dakhani Taj or Taj of the Deccan.

description: One of the finest examples of how a fort’s defence mechanism should be, Daulatabad Fort is a marvellous structure with glorious history attached to it. Perched on the conical hill of Deogiri, the original fort was built by the Yadava Dynasty at the end of the 12th century. Muhammad-bin-Tughluq annexed the fort town, changed its name from Deogiri to Daulatabad and shifted his capital from Delhi to this place. Later, the fort exchanged hands among Bahamani Rulers, Nizam Shah Dynasty, Mughals, Marathas and Nizams of Hyderabad. Accessible by climbing neary 600 steps, the fort welcomes the visitors through a dark passage called andheri, built strategically to make the access difficult for the enemy. Three concentric fortification circles, called Amber Kot, Maha Kot and Kala Kot, add to the fort’s invincible defence. Moat excavated from living rock has great width and depth which further gave the fort advantages over opponents. The structures inside the fort complex consist of beautiful palaces like Rang Mahal, Chini Mahal and Baradari (octagonal in shape). Chand Minar, also called the Victory Tower, is one of the highest towers in India. Hathi Kund (Elephant tank) and Saraswati Bawdi (stepped-well) have their own exclusive charm. Among the most striking features of the fort are a number of cannons installed in all parts. Mendha Tope with numerous inscriptions and cascabel in the shape of a ram is the most impressive one. Breathtaking views from the fort keep the visitors elated.

description: Guarding its virgin beauty and unspoiled charm, Ganapatipule Beach is possibly the most charming stretch along the Konkan Coast. Fine quality silvery sand and fringes boasting of splendidly grown palms, mangoes, cashews, betel nuts and jackfruits vegetations make this calm and clean beach the perfect escape from chaotic life. Cool breeze and warm sunrays fascinate the mind and soul as much as the melodious sea waves. Some thousands of years old temple of Lord Ganesha is the reason for huge flocks of tourists to throng the beach. The self-manifested (swayambhu) idol of the lord is one of the Ashtha (8) Ganapatis found across India and is believed to fulfil every wish. Ganapatipule Beach offers great adventures in the form of kayaking and motorboats, water scooters, row and pedal boats and aero boats. Climb the hill overlooking the beach and you can savour some breathtaking views of the place. Some good restaurants at the beach promise mouth-watering Marathi cuisines. The place is ideal for honeymoon couples, pilgrims as well as nature lovers.

description: Located on the Elephanta Island, the Elephanta Caves are created using the solid basalt rock. Built on a height of 60,000 sq.ft., the caves symbolise the celestial mountain dwelling of Lord Shiva – Mount Kailash. Elephanta Islandthat houses the famous Elephanta Caves is named after a colossal elephant (popularly known as Gharapuri) that was once found on the Elephanta Island. Elephanta Caves are declared as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Known to be one of the biggest historical attractions of Mumbai, Elephanta Caves date back to the 5th century BC. Any trip to Mumbai is incomplete, if you miss the wonderful Elephanta Caves. These impressive caves comprise of the main chamber, courtyards and numerous eye-catching shrines. Contrived in correspondence with the focal points worked out in a geometric Mandala, the design represents the huge field of energy. The large hall inside the cave temple has nine engraved panels that portray Lord Shiva in different moods. Although, very few details are available on the artists who carved theseglorious temples, it is clear that the structure is created out of gauzy rock and primitive tools. Other places of tourist interest near Elephanta Caves are the Gateway of India, Flora Fountain, Crawford Market, Prince of Wales Museum, Marine Drive and Hanging Gardens.

description: Perched on one of the highest points of Western Ghats, about 4.472 ft. above sea level, is the historical Purandar (or Purandhar) Fort. The place actually accommodates two forts, the bigger one is Purandar and the smaller one being Vajragad, connected to each other. Though, established in the 13th century by Bahamani Sultans, the fort attained peak glory under the rule of Shivaji, the greatest Maratha warrior. Attractions at the fort are divided into two levels, Machi (the lower one) and Ballekilla (the upper one). Apart from huge bastions and gates, the fort boasts of temples dedicated to Purandareshwar (the local deity) and Kedareshwar (dedicated to Lord Shiva). An old church is among the most remarkable spots inside the fort. However, it is the lavish greenery and breathtaking views from the top that mesmerize the visitors. The fort usually gets engulfed in mist which adds to the delight of being there. Don’t miss the opportunity to enjoy a thrilling trekking adventure to the fort. However, don’t forget to take along mineral water and raingear. Food stalls are available at the mid-point of the trek at Binni Darwaaza. Be careful in monsoons when the trek trails become slippery. Visitors need to enrol their names and vehicle number at an army check post on the way to the fort.

description: No trip to Mumbai is complete without visiting the lively and entertaining Chowpatty Beach. Also called Girgaum Chowpatty or Chaupati Beach, it remains occupied with flocks of local residents and tourists. The most striking aspect of the beach is the huge arrays of stalls selling mouth-watering snacks and street food including pav bhaji, panipuri, bhelpuri, vada pav and ragda patties and the local kulfi (ice-cream). Camel and horse riding escalate the fun one can enjoy at this beach. There are fun-filled rides, like merry-go-round and ferry wheels, that attract children and adults alike. Every corner of the beach boasts of balloon-sellers, fortune-tellers, snake charmers and street performers. Enjoy a monkey dance show or a magic show, common forms of entertainment at Chowpatty. The sandy beach is also popular for the end-day celebrations of Ganesha Chaturthi when idols of Lord Ganesha in all sizes are immersed in the sea water. Visit the beach at the sunset time to savour majestic beauty of the place.

description: Around 200 caves dating back to the period from the 1 st to 3 rd centuries spread over 4 different hills near the town of Junnar which served as an important trading centre during that era. Most of the caves are of Buddhist origin and revered by the followers as great Buddhist pilgrimage sites. Lenyadri Caves (around 40 in number) are the most popular ones, known for the splendid chaitya griha (a type of Buddhist shrine in Cave 6) and Girijatmaj Temple (one of the 8 holy shrines of Ganesha in the state) dedicated to the Hindu God Ganesha (inside Cave 7). The second group is known as Shivneri Caves (around 50 in number). These are further divided into four groups according to the directions they face. Tulja Caves (around 11 in number) form the third group which mainly consists of Buddhist viharas (dwelling places of the monks) and also a Hindu temple dedicated to Goddess Tulja. Manmod Caves belong to the fourth group and are further divided into Bhimasankar, Ambika and Bhutling groups of caves. Verandas, chapels, halls and pillars are among the main highlights of these caves some of which also contain some interesting inscriptions and paintings. Surrounded by lavish greenery, these caves promise great peace for mind.

description: One of the cleanest and pollution-free beaches in the whole of India, Alibaug Beach is well-known for its sand which is one shade black as compared to that found on other beaches in the region. Unlike other beaches of India’s western coast, it remains free from tourist crowds and offers excellent tranquillity. Views of Kolaba Fort that is located few metres away from the shore add to the delight of being here. Apart from taking leisurely walk along the sea, one can enjoy rides on tongas (horse carts). Filled with fresh and unpolluted air, the beach is dotted with coconut trees and is ideal for everyone, from playful kids to romantic couples and family tourists. There are street food vendors offering tangy snacks like vada-paav and bhelpuri and refreshing coconut water. Bathing and swimming are the fun-filled activities here. Volleyball and cricket can also be enjoyed at the beach. Warnings: Tides are strong and rough during monsoons and it is advised not to go deep into the water.

description: Maharashtra is home to many rock-cut monuments showcasing astonishing architectural excellence and religious significance. Bhaja (Bhaje) Caves, the group of 22 rock-cut caves, is among the prime examples of such tourist hotspots. Popular for its elaborated facades and numerous elements of Hinayana Buddhism, the caves are designated as the National Monument by the Archaeological Survey of India. Main highlights of these caves include horseshoe-arched entry points, chaityagriha (a type of prayer room), inscribes wooden beams, images of Lord Buddha and a number of Buddhist Stupas. Sculptures and intricate carvings all over the caves can leave one spellbound. Another highlight of the caves is the waterfall which comes alive during monsoons and adds beauty to the place. The trek to the caves is easy and offers ample opportunities of nature sightseeing. Believed to be located on an ancient trade route, these hundreds of years old caves are perfect to explore skilled Indian craftsmanship.

description: Maharashtra is home to many Buddhist caves that preserve hundreds of years old heritage and culture. A rocky hillside near Lonavala is home to one such set of rock-cut caves called Karla Caves. The most remarkable attraction of these caves is the Buddhist chaityagriha, acclaimed as India’s largest of its kind. With a base in the form of water jar and supported by 37 beautifully carved pillars, the shrine also boasts of elaborated figures of men, women and animals. A set of 16 excavations, these caves feature many other small chaitya grihas and viharas (dwelling places for monks). A gigantic lion pillar, which imitates the famous Ashoka Pillar, near the main chaitya griha is another spellbinding attraction. While some caves date back to the period from 2nd century BC to 2nd century AD, others were carved between 5th and 10th centuries AD. Intricately designed teak wood facades and horseshoe archways are the compelling aspects of the caves that are designated as protected monuments by the Archaeological Survey of India.

description: Serenity, beauty,cool breeze and pleasing sound of sea waves, Murud-Janjira is blessed with greenery and glittering sandy beaches. The pure and picture-perfect surrounding in Murud-Janjira entices visitors from across the world. A haven for adventure lover, visitors can enjoy variety of water-sport activities like parasailing, motorbike ride, banana ride, swimming and boating. Popular for its scintillating beaches, ancient forts, hot springs, swaying palm trees and majestic mountains, Murud-Janjira has many temples and churches spread across the place. The small fishing village, Murud is famous for its Janjira fort that is stationed in the middle of the sea. Visitors can take a boat ride to reach the fort and know the history of Maharashtra from the hired guide. Visitors can enjoy leisure activities like horseback ride, bullock cart ride or simply a romantic walk on the Murud beach. Tourists can explore other nearby attractions like Nandgaon, Kashid Beach, Ahmedganj Palace, Garambi Falls, Kasa Fort and the Janjira Caves.

description: Heaven for wildlife enthusiasts, Anerdam Wildlife Sanctuary is one of the richest natural wildlife habitats for a variety of aboriginal and endangered animal species and vegetation. Situated in Shirpur Tehsil of Dhule District in Maharashtra, the Anerdam Wildlife Sanctuary boasts a variety of flora and fauna like hiwar, palas, khair, bel, dhawda and babul. Abode to a number of native and migrant animals like jungle cats, chikaras, hyenas, peafs, corts and jackals, the sanctuary is a Shangri-la for wildlife fanciers. The sanctuary is also home to Monitor Lizards. Anerdam Wildlife Sanctuary is one of the richest sanctuaries in India. Sprawled over an area of 82.94 sq km. the sanctuary is also residence to exciting bird population. One can spot indigenous birds viz. Hammers, eagles, quails, owls, corts and cormorants. If you are lucky enough, you will also get a glimpse of migratory birds like stokes, Brahming ducks and cranes. You can also visit some of the places nearby namely Daulatabad Fort, Ellora Caves, Panchakki and Jayakwadi Bird Sanctuary.

description: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya, formerly known as Prince of Wales Museum is one of the premier museums of India. It was established in the 20th century by the Prince of Wales and hence gained its name. The museum is divided into three floors with several galleries displaying art, maritime, archeology and natural history. Its admirable architecture and an amazing collection of rare archeological artifacts, sculptures, and ancient artworks are spectacular. It has been awarded “The 2010 UNESCO Asia-Pacific Heritage Award’ for the conservation of rich cultural heritage. This most important landmark of Mumbai is a must visit for educational tours as it is a storehouse of knowledge.

description: The iconic symbol of Mumbai’s Gothic City grandeur, Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus is an historic railway station serving as the headquarters for the Indian Railways’ Central branch. Constructed over the time span of 10 years from 1878-88, it was originally called Victoria Terminus to honour Queen Victoria. Portraying the wonderful blend of Victorian Italianate Gothic Revival architecture and Mughal palace architecture, the heritage building is a part of UNESCO World Heritage Sites. Apart from the exclusive architectural elements like domes, towers, spires, pointed arches and turrets, the building also boasts of ornamental work and splendid carvings and sculptures of animals like lion, tiger, peacock, elephant and snake. The use of colourful tiles and stained window glasses further enhance the glory of this architectural marvel. Visitors can access the areas like Heritage Gallery and Star Chamber and the Railway Museum featuring old railway crockery, furniture and telephones on guided tours that cost INR 200 per head (INR 100 for students). Timing for this guided tour is from 03:00 p.m. to 05:00 p.m.

description: Clown Town was established as one of the first amusement park in the city of Kolkata. Targeting children of all age groups, the amusement park is well-known for its attractions like jump walls, bike rides and bungee run. Children are extremely attracted towards bungee jump as it is full of fun and excitement. Kids’ Grand Prix is another popular section where children can enjoy motor racing. Kiddies Kingdom is specifically meant for the children up to 5 years. The section has a variety of soft toys as well as the most popular Disney characters that instantaneously grab the attention of the young ones. Roller skates, skate boards, aquatic games and a mini golf course are other highlights for the small visitors. Adults accompanying children can also enjoy at lawns and areas meant for playing games like table tennis, ball and board, archery and carom. Foot loose is a dancing floor for the kids. The park also boasts of a refreshment area where fast food and soft drinks can be enjoyed.

description: One of the most coveted holiday spots in Mumbai, Essel World is famed as one of India’s largest amusement parks. The park amuses adults and kids equally with its thrilling rides. Located in the coastal village of Gorai, Essel World has the record of attracting more than 1.8 million visitors each year. Unfolded in 64 acres, Essel World showcases kids, adults and family rides under one roof, making it a fun-filled vacation destination for all the members of your family. Adult rides are the main attractions of the park that includes Shot-N-Drop, Top Spin, Hoola-Loop, Rainbow, Slippery Sultan, Thunder, Zyclone and Enterprise. The junior members of your family can enjoy the small yet amusing rides like Caterpillar, Boat-Ride, Big-Apple, Kangaroo Hop, Happy-Sky and Jr-Go-Kart etc. The electrifying family rides involve Copper Chopper, Aero Swinger, Aqua Dive, Zipper Dripper and Rocking Alley etc. The changing taste and growing demands of the present generation has urged the company to add a few more attractions. Some of the newly added ones are – a Skating Rink and a Laser Tag Arena. Other places of interest near Essel World are Gorai Beach and Water Kingdom.

description: Among the several colonial landmarks of Mumbai, Flora Fountain has something extremely graceful and attractive about itself. A masterpiece of New Classical and Gothic Revival architectural styles, it boasts of exquisite sculpted art with highest sense of old world charm. Dedicated to Flora, the Roman Goddess, the monument was built in 1864. Since then, it has a count among the heritage treasures which Mumbai proudly showcases to the world. It is indeed an eye-pleasing moment to view the fountain in actions after sunset. Numerous shopping venues and vendors in the close proximity attract the visitors. Flora Fountain is a part of the Hutatma Chowk which is surrounded by numerous heritage wonders like the Gateway of India, Central Telegraph Office, Old Secretariat and many specimen of British Victorian architecture. Spending a full day exploring the classic architecture is certainly fascinating.

description: Constructed to commemorate the first visit of King George V and Queen Mary to India, the Gateway of India served as the entry for the British Viceroys and Governors and also as the final exit of the British as India got independence. Constructed from 1911-1924 using yellow basalt and reinforced concrete, the grab structure combines Hindu and Muslim architectural styles and is a perfect example of Indo-Saracenic construction style. The place bustles with tourist activities throughout the day. Tourists visit the monument to savour its old world charm and fascinating magnificence and also to enjoy mesmerizing views of the Arabian Sea. As many as five jetties exist nearby, serving interesting tourist routes to Elephanta Caves and Alibaug. Getting photographed in front of the monument is a favourite among the tourists. Another wonderful highlight is a ride in Victoria, horse-driven gilded carriages reminiscent of the British era. Probably the most iconic landmark of Mumbai, the Gateway of India also has nearby attractions like Statues of Chhatrapati Shivaji and Swami Vivekanand, Flora Fountain and the world-famous Taj Hotel.

description: Haji Ali Dargah and Mosque form a splendid whitewashed complex located spectacularly amidst Arabian Sea, some 500 yard off the Mumbai shoreline. Housing the tomb of the Sufi saint Pir Haji Ali Shah Bukhari and an adjoining mosque, the place greets people from all religions and faiths. It is believed that when the saint died on his way to Mecca, his body placed in a casket floated to this place and hence the dargah was constructed. A mosque was added later to the complex. Built in the impressive Indo-Islamic architecture, the structure boasts of ornamental gateway and ceilings with rich mirror work depicting Arabian patterns and 99 names of Allah. Recently added pure marble work has raised the overall splendour of the complex. There are separate entrance points for men and women. Thursday and Friday experience huge crowds of ardent followers and devotees. The complex is accessible only during low tide, as the pathway connecting it to the shoreline submerges in water during high tide. Visit the place early morning during the sunrise and watch it fabulously bathing in the orange light. Be sure to wear traditional clothes and follow the religious conduct when visiting the dargah. Outstanding peace and serenity and regular Sufi music performances at this sacred place create irresistibly engrossing environment.

description: Among several temples established by the International Society for Krishna Consciousness (ISKCON) around the world, one in the Juhu region of Mumbai attracts hordes of devotees and tourists. Located close to the Juhu Beach, the astounding white marble temple stands in a huge complex which also houses an auditorium, a restaurant and a spacious guest house for the devotees to stay. Dedicated to Lord Krishna, the temple is also popular by the names like Sri Sri Radha Rasabihari Temple and Hare Rama Hare Krishna Temple. The construction of the temple at a place which was once nothing more than a dense forest is indeed a matter of surprise. The dream was conceived by Swami Prabhupada who purchased the land and led the temple’s construction in 1978. Beautiful paintings and exotic interiors along with marble statues of various Hindu deities create spellbinding ambiance inside the temple. Constant chanting of Mahamantra, the divine syllable, fills the atmosphere with utmost spirituality. Attending prayers and bhajans organized on daily basis can be an experience of the lifetime. You can also visit the temple during Janamashthmi and other special occasions when exclusive cultural programmes are held.

description: India’s liveliest beach, Juhu Beach bustles with life, activities and unending entertainment. The beach has many Bollywood celebrities living in its close proximity and one may get lucky to watch ongoing film shooting which is common at the beach. Juhu Beach is popular for its food vendors serving tangy and mouth-watering snacks like pav bhaji, panipuri, bhelpuri and a variety of desserts. There are some very renowned Italian restaurants near the beach that serve delicious seafood preparations. When at Juhu, you can watch dancing monkeys, snake charmers and other sorts of street performers that keep the crowds amused. You may also like to have a camel or elephant ride at the beach. Another well-known aspect of the beach is that one can watch the majestic views of the sun setting across the Arabian Sea. Yoga and meditation are other good to perform activities here. Other attractions of the beach include ISKCON Temple and statues of Indian freedom fighters. The beach is also famous for being on the airplane take-off route, offering amazing views of the planes just after they rake-off or are ready to land.

description: Set up over 140 years back, Mahalaxmi dhobi Ghat is a veritable land mark of Mumbai city life. This massive open air laundry where dirty clothes from all over the city are brought and washed manually by washermen in long rows of flogging stones is one of the top 10 attractions in Mumbai. Formed originally to serve the dominant English and Parsi population of the city, this facility is owned by the municipal council, which is let out to the washermen, many of them in this business for decades. There are over 1026 open-air concrete wash pens in this dhobi ghat and the clock work precision of the seasoned hands and the traditional washing methods have made it one of the unique experiences in the world. The spectacular glimpses of thousands of clothes on the cloths line would sum up the sheer amount of action that this washing ghat witnesses.

description: Served as a residence to Mahatma Gandhi from 1917 to 1934, Mani Bhavan Gandhi Museum is a two-storey house symbolic of true patriotism. Deemed as the huge and most impressive mansion from pre-independence time, this house was transformed into a museum. Popular as the memorial of the Father of the Nation, Mani Bhavan Gandhi Museum houses the famous Old Charkha of Gandhiji. The museum has a picture gallery that showcases photographs, slogans, posters and items related to Gandhi’s life. Mani Bhavan Gandhi Museum houses a reference library that consists of more than 50,000 books, film and recordings related to Gandhi. Visitors can enjoy the special screening of films made on Gandhi in the auditorium inside the museum. A tour to this museum will give you serene and wonderful experience. Other nearby attractions includes, Shree Siddhivinayak Temple, Gateway of India, Marine Drive, Chowpatty Beach and Marve Beach etc.

description: Dubbed as ‘Mini-Goa,’ Manori Beach is one of the most visited and finest picnic spots in Mumbai. Positioned in a small fishing village of Northern Mumbai – Manori, the beach is popular for family outings and late night party buffs. The Manori village was once under the governance of Portuguese, whose remnants are still found in the hamlet through its churches and chapels. Outlined with orchards full of cashew nut trees, Manori Beach is believed to be one of the most beautiful and serene beaches in the city. Famous as a connecting link between Mumbai city and amusement parks like Essel World and Water Kingdom, this beach reminds of Goa through its culture and nearby settlement. The best thing about this beach, it is clean and less crowded therefore tranquil. Manori beach is surrounded by other wonderful attractions like Marve and Gorai Beaches. You can take a ferry ride from Manori to these beautiful beaches.

description: Marve beach is one of the unspoilt tranquil beaches in Mumbai, which is tucked away from the maddening rush of the city. It is a perfect place to enjoy a quiet evening away from the Mumbai crowd and for the views of rising sun. Situated in Mumbai’s western suburbs of Malad, it is one of the smaller beaches ensuring a calmer atmosphere than most tourist locations. INS(Indian Naval Base) Hamla camp is located here and it is a great sight to see the INS Hamla soldiers training on the beach in the mornings. Sunset time is the best time to enjoy the beauty of this beach. An ancient church and surroundings adorned with Cashew nut tress make the beach view exquisite. Numerous tourists visit this beach in the evenings to stroll across the golden sands. Hawkers’ selling hot items to eat and drink is a commons sight on this beach. Some of them even sell souvenirs and showpieces made of shells collected on the beach. Fishermen collecting fishes in baskets are a common sight on this beach. At the northern end of this beach, there are ferry services available to Essel World and Water Kingdom. Ferry boats are available to other beaches nearby like Gorai Beach, Uttan and to Manori Island. The beach is unsafe for swimming due to sinking sand and fast current.

description: Prince of Wales Museum, now known as Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya, is known for housing more than 50,000 artefacts portraying the rich heritage of India. Established in the early 20th century to commemorate the visit of King George V to India, the museum building boasts of remarkable Indo-Saracenic Architecture, inspired by the royal Mughal Architecture. The main three sections of exhibits at the museum include Arts, Archaeology and Natural History. The Art section has spectacular collections of over 2,000 miniature paintings from various schools of art across India, along with many palm leaf manuscripts, decorative artefacts in jade, ivory, silver, gold and other metals and stone sculptures. The Archaeological exhibits include artefacts from Indus Valley Civilization and many powerful dynasties and empires. Terracotta figures and coins are among the top highlights. The Natural History section exhibits India’s rich flora and fauns, including many birds, mammals, reptiles and other species. In addition, the park has galleries for maritime heritage, forestry, armoury, textiles and European oil paintings. Students and other sections of visitors can obtain discount on the museum tickets. Additional charges are imposed for audio guides.

description: Sprawling over 44.80 acres of land near the western border of the ‘Orange City’ - Nagpur, Ambazari gardenis decorated with an adulterated big lake of the same name. Ambazari Lake is one of the 11 lakes in Nagpur and regarded as one of the largest and gorgeous lakes in the city. Ambazari Lake is fenced with several tall green mango trees, and gets its name from Amba means mango in Marathi. The Nag River of the city springs up from this lake. The lake serves as a popular getaway spot for locals and tourists during weekends and summer evenings. Visitors can enjoy a wonderful boat ride with the entire family. The Ambazari theme park located beside the Ambazari Lake offers beautiful view of musical fountain, amusement games, and different electrical rides, which entertains adults and children equally. The musical fountain presents a dramatic concord of music and water, amusing both parents and children. Different entertainment options are available for kids like Columbus boat ride, mini train ride, see-saw fun and the giant striking structure of a dinosaur at the entrance. The virgin beauty of the lake and garden enhances the beauty of the city. You can also visit the nearby attractions viz. Dhamma Chakra Stupa, Futala Lake, Musical Garden, Bohra Masjid and Ramtek Fort Temple.

description: Home to one of the 12 Jyotirlingas (where Lord Shiva is worshipped as an endless pillar of divine light), Baidyanath is one of the top Hindu pilgrimage destinations of East India. Popularly known as Baidyanath Dham and Baba Dham, the temple complex consists of one main temple dedicated to Lord Shiva and 21 other temples dedicated to different deities. The temple holds significance from the legend of demon king Ravana who was a devout follower of Shiva. The main temple is a stone pyramidal structure which houses the cylindrical shaped Shiva Lingam. Other highlights of the temple include Chandrakanta Mani (a jewel in the form of lotus) and Punchsula (five knives in the shape of trident). Visiting the temple during popular festivals can be an extraordinary experience. One may also visit other nearby attractions like Nandan Pahar, Basukinath Shiva Temple, Taponath Mahadeva Temple, Shivaganga Pool, Maa Kali Shaktipeeth and Trikuth Parvat.

description: The Balaji Mandir, Nagpur is one of the most prominent temples in the state of Maharashtra. Its location is at the Seminary Hills which give a picturesque surrounding to this holy place. This temple is devoted to the important deities of Hindu religion viz. Lord Balaji and Lord Kartikeya. Hindu mythology believes that Lord Kartikeya is an important leader in the army of Hindu Gods and therefore, the temple is also known as Sri Balaji-Karthikeyan Temple. The speciality of this temple is the enclosure which is 60 feet long with huge idols of Lord Balaji and Lord Kartikeya poise from a single petal vimana (Ekdala Vimana) in a unique three levels arrangement which displays the South Indian temple architecture. The temple is surrounded by undisturbed beauty of the nature which adds to the serenity of the temple arena. It’s a must visit place for tourists since the temple showcases a peaceful place to pray along with a splendid view of Nagpur from the top of the temple.

description: Never in the world’s history had a place witnessed mass conversion of such kind. Deekshabhoomi (land of religious ordaining) wrote history when Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar, the inventor of Indian Constitution, chose the place in Nagpur to convert from Hinduism to Buddhism in1956. What follows every year is the mass conversion to Buddhism, attended by uncountable people from all over the world. To commemorate the historic place, world’s largest hollow Buddhist Stupa was constructed here. A two-storey structure in hemispherical shape with the capacity of several thousands, the stupa attracts Buddhist followers from various countries. The use of Dholpur sandstone, marble and granite and portrayal of Buddhist symbols like Ashoka Chakras and statues of horses, elephants and lions add grandeur to this enthralling structure. Additional attractions include a Buddhist Vihara, housing a Bronze Buddha statue, and a sacred Bodhi Tree, a descendant of the world-famous Bodhi Tree in Sri Lanka. Dhamma Chakra Pravartan Din is the annual occasion of mass conversion when the place welcomes people of all faiths.

description: Located in the heart of Nagpur city,MaharajBagh and zoo stands on the garden of the Bhonsle and Maratha rulers of the city. The MaharajBagh has a Botanical Garden and a zoo that has many birds and animals. The garden is popular amongst naturalists and school children who take keen interest on the exhibits of the Botanical Garden. There is also a play area for children and view points from where the visitors can enjoy panoramic views around. Apart from a cacti and fern house, this garden also has a nursery where the visitors can buy saplings and seeds of plants. Other attractions include a cascading waterfall, the bamboo museum and a sprawling rose garden that features over 600 species roses. The herbal garden is another essential stopover here, where you can learn about different types of herbs and their healing properties. During the winter season, many migratory birds visit the garden adding up to its beauty. MaharajBagh offers something for everyone in the family. No matter whether you are here to relax in the lap of nature or to know about the diverse wild life of the country, you can be assured of a memorable experience.

description: Nagpur Central Museum, also known as Ajab Bangla and Madhyavarthi Sangrahalay, was constructed by the British way back in 1863. The museum is hone to many rare and interesting artefacts, some of them collected from the sites of Indus Valley Civilization. Apart from artefacts, the museum has other exhibits in the form of coins, sculptures, inscriptions. The 6 galleries displaying these artefacts include Art, Archaeology, Anthropology, Geology, Industry and Natural History. Among the most popular exhibits at the museum are the stuffed and preserves birds, mammals and reptiles. The visitors can find great deal of information about the history and biodiversity of Central India at the museum. There is a library within the premises and a newly introduced cafeteria. The museum has been recently renovated and is surrounded by beautiful gardens. You may get to attend international film shows, lectures and other events at the museum.

description: Popular as one of jewels of Nagpur, Sitabuldi Fort (also known as Sitabardi or Seetabuldee Fort) overlooks the city from the top of a hillock. The fort and the area around it span over the twin hills called Badi Tekri (big hill) and Choti Tekri (small hill). Believed to be named after two brothers Shitlaprasad and Badriprasad, the fort came into prominence in the 19th century under the British rule. The fort served as the battleground in 1817 in which the British defeated the Marathas. In 1857, the British executed their enemies from Mysore at the fort. One can find the graveyard of the English soldiers and a mosque dedicated to the Muslim martyrs. Mahatma Gandhi was also imprisoned for a brief period at the fort and one can visit the cell where he was kept. Today, the fort is under the control of the Indian Army. It opens for the public only on three days – Republic Day (January 26), Independence Day (August 15) and Maharashtra Day (May 01).

description: The area between the districts of Bhandara and Gondia is home to the Nagzira Wildlife Sanctuary. Known for its mesmerizing natural beauty, lush green landscapes and significant population of tigers and other wild animals, the sanctuary was recently made a part of the prestigious Project Tiger. Jungle safari trips to the sanctuary are conducted in jeeps and on elephants. Apart from tigers, one may come across the species like panther, bison, sloth bear, nilgai, chital, wild boar, wild dog, jackal, palm civet, giant flying squirrel and many more. The sanctuary is also famous for its birds, reptiles, fishes and insects as well as the fabulous geological features like picturesque lakes and rock formations (of slates, chlorites and other stones). Mixed forests and huge expanses of teaks, bamboos and meadows add to the sanctuary’s inimitable grandeur. The park closes from mid-June to September. Visitors need to shell out extra fees for guides.

Taxis can be hired for the journey over well-maintained roads. The closest bus stand is at Sakoli (about 18 km. away).""}

description: Also known as Trirashmi Caves, after the name of the hillock they are located on, Pandu Lena Caves consist of 24 Buddhist caves dating between the 2nd century BC and 2nd century AD, further extended in design up till 12th century AD. Cave number 18 is the only Chaitya (prayer hall) with stupa, while remaining 23 caves represent Buddhist viharas (monasteries). Detailed inscriptions found in many of these caves provide huge information about the rulers and Buddhist followers who donated and assisted in the process of carving these caves. Apart from carvings and inscriptions, there are huge idols of Buddha and many other sculptures that are enough to leave one flabbergasted with delight. Cave 3 and Cave 10 are extremely admirable for their ornamented pillars. Water tanks carved from the rocks are the additional features and boast of the skills that people had centuries ago. It is a pleasure to have wonderful views of the entire city from the top of the hillock. Try visiting the caves at the earliest in the morning to watch their peak splendour under the rising sun.

description: Nestled amidst natural greenery, 36 km. from Nashik district; lies the magnificent Trimbakeshwar Temple in the village of Trimbak. This divine Shiva temple is situated at the source of the river Godavari. Trimbakeshwar is also abode to one of the twelve Jyotirlingas of the Hindu God Shiva. According to ancient legends, God Shiva pleased with the penance of Gautam Rishi blessed the earth with river Godavari. Devotional visitors coming to visit this prime Shiva temple usually take a dip in the holy waters of Godavari before entering the temple precincts. Interestingly, the temple trustees offer no VIP passes. The visitors going for general darshan need not follow the dress code, but those male devotees wishing to touch the pristine ‘pindi’ of lord Trimbakeshwar have to wear a specific cloth ‘sovale’. Unfortunately, like many Hindu temples in India, ladies are not allowed to touch the ‘pindi’. Mondays are special days and considered very holy for worshipping Lord Shiva. In Trimbakeshwar; the idol is carried by the priests in a palanquin around the shrine. Likewise, the yearly spiritual festivity during ‘Shivratri’ is conducted with great devotional fervour by thousands of visiting pilgrims coming from all over the world.

description: The most coveted spot for bird lovers and hikers, Karnala Bird Sanctuary is positioned in Panvel, outside Mumbai. Although a small refuge, the place houses more than 150 resident bird species and 37 migratory bird species. Bird watchers can spot three rare bird varieties like Malabar Trogon, Ashy Minivet and three toed Kingfisher etc. Nuzzled in the heart of Sahyadri hills, the sanctuary consists of moist deciduous forest. Monsoon is regarded as the ideal time for spotting the paradise flycatcher, Malabar whistling thrush and magpie robin. Bird lovers seeking the glimpse of migratory birds, can choose to visit Karnala Bird Sanctuary during winter. The red breasted flycatcher, the blue-headed rock-thrush, the blue throat and blackbird bunch to the sanctuary during winter. Visitors can enjoy the nearby attractions like Karnala Pinnacle, Lonavala, Khopoli, Karnala Fort and Matheran.

description: Spread along the picturesque Radhanagri backwaters over the end hills of Sahydri range of Western Ghats, Dajipur Village is home to Dajipur Wildlife Sanctuary (also known as Bison Sanctuary). Lavishly green mountains and valleys sheltering beautiful lakes and soaring waterfalls enchant the visitors with its inimitable landscapes. Valiant and bold bison (Indian gaur) is the main attraction of the sanctuary which also offers home to wild deer, wild boar, sloth bear, sambar, giant squirrel and around 6-12 tigers. Tourists can also find birds like quails, bulbul, hornbills, doves, warbles and many other attention-grabbing species. Vegetation in the sanctuary consists of karvi, chandala, jambul, hirda, kumbha, banana, murudsheng, bamboo and many species of climbing plants. Sunset Point is a vantage point promising splendid views of nature. Inside the village, there is a hall where pug marks of wild animals cast in plaster can be explored. Some of the must-visit destinations around Dajipur include Radhanagri Sanctuary, Sagareshwar Sanctuary, Laxmi Talao, Kal Bhairao Temple, Radhanagri Dam and coastal plains of Konkan.

description: Jayakwadi Dam built over River Godavari led to the creation of Nath Sagar Lake which became the dwelling place for many local bird species. Over many years, the reservoir has slowly emerged as a major draw for the migratory birds from within India and also from the international locations. This has earned it the recognition as Jayakwadi (or Jaikwadi) Bird Sanctuary. The lake boasts of nearly 30 natural islands with many trees that offer habitat to these birds. Among the 200 species of birds spotted here, 70 are migratory out of which nearly 45 hail from foreign regions. These include the likes of flamingos, cranes, pintails, glossy ibis, pochards teals, Brahmany duck and many more. In addition, there are more than 50 species of fish that can be found in the lake’s water. Eye-pleasing aquatic vegetation is an added attraction, while the nearby Sant Gyaneshwar Udhyan (garden) can also be visited. Don’t forget to carry binoculars and cameras to sightsee and capture the amazing beauty of the place.

description: Often regarded as the largest of various forts in the Deccan, Panhala Fort was constructed in 1178 AD by Bhoja II of Shilahara Dynasty. The fort later exchanged hands between many powerful dynasties and empires like Yadavas, Bahamani, Bijapur, Mughals, Marathas and British. Located in the folds of Sahyadri Range of mountains, the fort is admired for its impressive defence mechanisms, about 7 km. long fortifications, bastions and many unique structures. The top highlights of the fort include three double gateways of Teen Darwaza, Wagh Darwaza and Char Darwaza, huge granaries of Ambarkhana and Dharma Kothi, pleasure pavilion of Sajja Kothi, temples of Someshwar and Ambabai and a number of mausoleums. In addition, there are structures like Rajdindi Bastion and Andhar Bavadi that were used as secret places by the soldiers during seize attempts by the enemy forces. More than 100 check posts and tunnels added to the fort’s security against enemies. Roughly triangular in layout, the fort is located about 845 m. above the sea level and offers breathtaking views of the valley.

description: Built as a symbol of charity by Sultan Muhammed Shah Aga Khan III, Aga Khan Palace gained historical importance with due course of time. Popular as a monument, a museum and an architectural marvel today, it was the place where Mahatma Gandhi and his associates were detained during the notable Quit India Movement in 1942. Designated as the monument of national importance, the palace is popular for Mahatma Gandhi Memorial (where the ashes of the greatest Indian leader rest) and Samadhis (cenotaphs) of Kasturba Gandhi (Gandhiji’s wife) and Mahadev Desai (Gandhiji’s personal secretary). Constructed in majestic royal style, the palace boasts of eye-pleasing Italian architectural elements and is surrounded by beautiful green lawns and gardens. Nearness to River Mula makes the place even more exciting. Main attractions inside the heritage building include picture gallery and museum (with pictures, letters and items used by Gandhiji on display), library and documentation centre and a shop selling khadi stuff (mark of Gandhiji’s freedom struggle). The monument is visited by flocks of tourists on occasions like Mahatma Gandhi’s Birth Anniversary (October 2), Death Anniversary (January 30), Independence Day (August 15) and Republic Day (January 26).
weeklyoff: Open 7 days a week. Close on National Holidays.

description: Bund Garden, also known as Mahatma Gandhi Udhyan, is a calm and beautiful tourist spot in Pune. Located on the bank of Rivers Mula and Mutha, the garden was originally conceived as an idea to construct a mini dam that would help farmers with irrigation. Today, it is a popular picnic spot where one can enjoy nature’s calmness and exquisiteness. Boating is a popular activity at the backwaters adjoining the park area. It is also a well-known destination among bird-watchers who get to see a variety of migratory bird species here. Winter months are most suitable for bird watching while monsoon months are ideal to experience nature’s glory at full bloom. Jogging, horse riding and regular magic shows add to the numbers of visitors to the garden. One can also visit Aga Khan Palace connected to the garden by a bridge.

description: Named after a reputed halwai (sweet-maker), Dagadusheth Halwai Ganapati Temple is among the most popular temples of Lord Ganesha (Ganapati) in the state of Maharashtra. The huge idol of Ganesha (fondly called Bappa) is visible from the road itself and is embellished with loads of gold ornaments. The temple turns into a fascinating venue during the celebrations of Ganesh Chaturthi. Dagadusheth Gadve got the idol sculpted on the advice of his guru after he lost his son. His sweets shop, named Kaka Halwai, has today turned into a popular franchise. Ardent devotees offer money and jewellery in huge amounts at the temple. Dagadusheth Halwai Ganapati Temple Trust uses these offerings in a number of philanthropy works. Tuesdays experience huge crowds of visitors at the temple. It is worth mentioning that one needs to undergo strict security check before entering the temple. Mobile phones, cameras and bags and baggage are not allowed inside and can be stores in the lockers. The elaborated aartis (prayers) at different times of the day are worth attending.

description: Osho Ashram, better known as Osho International Meditation Resort, Osho Commune International or simply Osho Pune, was established by Bhagwan Rajneesh (popularly known as Osho), the world-famous mystic and spiritual guru. The ashram has gained reputation as a facility offering life-affirming meditation courses, attracting thousands of visitors from every corner of the world every year. The campus is spread over 32 acre, away from the chaotic and messy city life. The meditation courses available at the ashram include Kundalini, Nadabrahma, Nataraj and Dynamic meditation, each promising stress busting and spiritual uplifting. Osho multiversity programs are joined by many and are counted among the world’s most popular meditation courses. In addition, the campus has many facilities like swimming pool, spa, sauna, massage parlours, sports courts and the very scenic Osho Teerth Park. Those who stay at the ashram can enjoy an intriguing nightlife through cinema, theatre, classical music, parties and dance meditations. Visitors can buy tickets to take the silent tours across the campus. Those willing to join needs to register and undergo an HIV test to confirm their eligibility. Children are strictly prohibited at the ashram. Meditation courses can be joined for different durations by purchasing daily meditation stickers.

description: A palace fort located in Pune, Shaniwar Wada which was constructed in 1746 by Bajirao Peshwa is one the famous monuments in the city. Embellished with gorgeous courtyards,impressive fountains and huge audience halls, Shaniwar Wada exemplifies the proficient and delicate architecture of Maratha craftsmen. The main attraction of this palace is its approximately 21 feet main door that faces north. Other interesting designs include its various intricately carved gates like Dilli Darwaza, Mastani Darwaza, Ganesh Darwaza and Khidki Darwaza etc. Popular as the most beautiful and important landmarks of Pune, Shaniwar Wada have certain paintings that portraythe scenes from Hindu epics like Mahabharata and Ramayana. The greeting garden and city centre located inside the palace depicts the luxurious side of Shaniwar Wada. Apart from the beauty, Shaniwar Wada also holds Sound and Light Show in Marathi from 7:15 p.m. to 8:10 p.m. and English show from 8:15 p.m. to 9:10 p.m.The Sound and Light show costs about INR 25 per person. Visitors to this place can also explore nearby attractions like Raja Dinkar Kelkar Museum, Aga Khan Palace, Pashan Lake, Sinhagad Fort and Empress Botanical Gardens.

description: Popular for its Shani Temple, Shani Shinganapur is tiny yet unique village situated in the Indian state of Maharashtra. Famous for having no doors and locks in any house, the village is said to be protected by the God of power - Shani. Visited by thousands of devotees every day, the village does not have a temple structure. There ismerean open-air platform that houses the idol of Shanidev. Only male devotees are allowed to worship the idol. The Shani idol in the village can be worshipped from 5:00 a.m. to 10:00 p.m. In order to worship the idol, male devotees should first take bath in open and wear dhoti without upper garment. Shani Shinganapur is usually crowded on Saturdays, because it is considered to be the favourite day of Lord Shanaishwara. Visitors to Shani Shinganapur can also visit nearby attractions like Shirdi, Ranjangaon, tomb of Sant Shri Udasi Baba and Shri Dattatraya Temple.

description: Shridi Sai Baba Temple was established in the year 1922 and since then it attracts devotees of all caste and religion. People come here to pay their homage to the most popular Indian spiritual saint Shri Sai Baba. The beautiful shrine was built over the Samadhi of this great master who taught the ethics of love, contentment, forgiveness and inner peace to all his devotees. It is strongly believed that Sai Baba’s body manifested immense energy which is moving the faith in many mysterious ways. This place is known to have the largest solar cooker in the world and can cook 20,000 meals every day for pilgrims. One can also visit the Sai Museum where all the personal belongings of this saint are preserved. There are many tales which describes the miracles performed by this spiritual guru which deepens one’s faith in his spirituality.

description: Sinhagad Fort, or the Lion’s Fort, served as a strategic military outpost for the Marathas, particularly for Shivaji. Located on a hill at the height of about 1,312 m. in the Bhuleswar Range of the picturesque Sahyadri Mountains, the fort is a famous trekking and picnic spot, apart from being an historical attraction. The top attractions inside the fort complex include memorials dedicated to Maratha warriors, old temples, military stables and a brewery. However, it is the breathtaking beauty of the place which leaves the visitors fascinated. If you plan to visit the fort after monsoons (September-October), you will be greeted by amazing greenery all around. Steep slopes and limited access (via only two gates – Kalyan Darwaza and Pune Darwaza) helped the fort to stay invincible for centuries. Avoid visiting the fort during monsoons due to extremely slippery surface.

description: Designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, Ellora Caves in Aurangabad are renowned for 34 impressive caves that are carved out of volcanic rock. Locally famous as ‘Verul Leni,’ it is the pride of Maharashtra. Displaying the flawless and impeccable illustration of art and architecture, Ellora Caves define the concept of ‘Unity in Diversity’ in India. Out of the 34 caves in Ellora, 17 are dedicated to Hindus, 14 to Buddhists and remaining to Jains. Ellora is home to the huge 8th century Kailashnath Temple (located in Cave 16) that portrays the scenes from the holy books of Ramayana and Mahabharata. The temple is created out of a single solid block of stone. The caves depict the harmony that exists between Hindu, Buddhist, Jains and other religions. Some of the highlights of Ellora Caves, which visitors shouldn’t miss on their trip, are Cave 2, Cave 6, Cave 11 and 12, Vishwakarma Cave, Hindu Caves and Jain Caves.

description: Great religious significance is associated with Ghrishneshwar Temple, it being one of the 12 extremely revered Jyotirlings dedicated to Lord Shiva. An interesting mythological legend states that an ardent devotee of Shiva named Kusuma would daily worship the lord by immersing Shiva Lingam in a water tank. She continued with the practice even after losing her son and this pleased Shiva who appeared in front of Kusuma. Not only did he turn her son alive, but also decided to exist at the place as a Jyotirling. The temple is therefore also known as Kusumeswarar. The most enthralling feature of the temple is its soaring 5-storey shikhara, carved majestically and decorated with impressive motifs. Originally the temple was built using pre-historic and other traditional construction styles. The present day temple was built in the 18th century by a local queen Ahilyabai Holkar. Apart from the sanctum sanctorum housing the divine Shiva Lingam, the temple has an antechamber and a pillared hall. The inner walls are embellished with inscriptions and paintings, promising beautiful sights. Monday being an auspicious day to worship Lord Shiva draws flocks of devotees to the temple. The fair organized on Mahashivaratri is worth attending.

description: World’s only floating national park, Keibul Lamjao National Park is an absolute wonder of nature. Spread over an area of about 40 sq. km., the park consists of floating masses of vegetation called phumdis over Lake Loktak. Brow-antlered deer, better known as Eld’s deer, locally as sangai and fondly as dancing deer, is the flagship specie of the park and also the state animal of Manipur. The park was declared a national park in 1977 with the main intention to protect and preserve the near-extinct deer. The park also accommodates other interesting wild species like hog deer, large Indian civet, wild boar, golden cat, common otter, flying fox, musk shrew and many types of fishes and reptiles. Avifauna here consists of hill myna, kingfishers, drongo, wagtail, hooded cranes, white-breasted waterhen and black kite among others. Aquatic flora of diverse varieties adds another dimension to the park’s beauty. Foreign tourists must obtain Restricted Area Pass (available at major metropolitans) to access Manipur and the park. The domestic tourists must also obtain Inner Line Permit for the trip.

description: Famous as one of the largest freshwater lakes in North East India, Loktak Lake houses a large collection of flora and fauna. Recognized as the world’s only ‘Floating Lake,’ this lake is designated as a Wetland of International Importance under the Ramsar Convention on 23 March, 1990. The rich biodiversity and unadulterated beauty surrounding this lake attracts visitors from around the world. Loktak Lake is habitat to about 116 bird species and 233 fauna species. Tourists can enjoy eating at the various food joints located nearby and enjoy nerve-chilling water sport activities. Other than the lake, visitors can also explore the attractions in vicinity like the Tourist Bungalow, Andro, Khonghampat Orchidarium, Langthabal, Keibul Lamjao National Park, the Manipur Zoological Garden and many more.

description: Located close to India’s border with Bangladesh, Baghmara Reserve Forest is the perfect combination of mesmerizing natural beauty and captivating wildlife. Hills and valleys covered with dense forests allow passages for many rivers and enthralling vantage points. Wildlife lovers get to see leopards, elephants (in huge herds), langurs, gaurs and many other mammals. The place is also popular for watching birds and sightseeing numerous species of butterflies. An exciting attraction of the reserve forest is the presence of pitcher plant. One can explore abundant flora while walking the natural trails inside the reserve. Baghmara Reserve Forest is located about 4 km. away from Baghmara town which serves as the headquarters of South Garo Hills. Few nearby attractions include Siju Caves, Siju Bird Sanctuary and Balpakram National Park.

description: Originally called Ka Kshaid Lai Pateng Khohsiew (meaning three-step waterfall), the enthralling Elephant Falls was named so by the British who found a nearby rock resembling an elephant. The rock doesn’t exist anymore, but the charm of this nature’s marvel definitely does. Its waters fall in three steps, the final step being the tallest and also the most fascinating one. Visitors to the waterfall can descend to the bottom through steep stairs, admiring the full journey of the fall. Roaring sounds of the water and cool atmosphere add to the thrill of being here. Green vegetation makes the overall scenery a visual treat for the eyes. There are some stalls selling hot tea and few shops selling interesting souvenirs. Be sure to carry cameras as the place promises some great photography opportunities.

description: Lady Hydari Park is one of the must see spots in Shillong. This spectacular garden is landscaped in Japanese style and has a well manicured lawn and flower beds with different plants including native plants and orchids. It is best known for its rich collection of rose plants. Widely popular among locals and tourists alike, this park is indeed a visual delight and a place to relax in the lap of Mother Nature. Named after the first lady of the province and the wife of the Governor of Assam, Lady Hydari, this park has about 73 species of birds, 140 species of reptiles and other mammals. Managed by the state forest department, this park has also a mini-zoo, the first of its kind in Shillong. It houses many animals and birds and also has a butterfly museum. There is a spacious and well kept children’s park nearby with swings, slips and other rides for children. Lady Hydari Park spreads over half a kilometre and is close to various other tourist attractions like the fabled Crinoline Falls. You can also enjoy a refreshing dip in the city’s only pool, Crinoline pool, which is also nearby.

description: Often regarded as the icon of the city of Pondicherry, Promenade Beach (or simply The Promenade) is a beautiful stretch of about 1.5 km. in length. The beach offers the amazing opportunity to savour the beauty of the Bay of Bengal. The Beach Road or Goubert Avenue is the road among the beach which offers an exceptional leisurely walk experience. The beach is also famous for housing many important heritage sites like the French War Memorial, the Heritage Town Hall, the Old Customs House and Chief Secretariat. In addition, there are many popular statues like those of Joan or Arc, Mahatma Gandhi and Dupleix. The Old Light House is an added attraction to the beach. Prefer to visit the beach during sunrise for some breathtaking views of the whole area. To enjoy walking along the avenue, prefer to visit in the evening when the traffic is restricted. The beach is not apt for swimming, but offers one to spend some great moments.

description: Rejuvenate your life with spiritual salvation at Aurobindo Ashram, one of the most prominent ashrams in India. Set up by Sri Aurobindo way back in 1926, the ashram was looked after by Madam Mirra Alfassa, fondly called The Mother or Maa by the followers. Offering an extremely peaceful environment to practice yoga and meditation, it attracts uncountable visitors, including huge numbers of foreigners, every year. Art-craft workshops, art gallery, ashram library, art house, handmade paper factory, weaving and embroidery departments and many other highlights of the ashram are worth exploring. There are accommodation facilities in the form of ashram-run guesthouses for those who wish to stay here and attain spirituality. Passes are required to stay, to dine and to participate in different activities. Visitors can also visit the cenotaphs (Samadhis) of Sri Aurobindo and The Mother. Guided tours, which begin at 08:15 am, can be joined to explore the ashram in the company of a guide. Additional Information: It is advisable to wear traditional or decent Indian clothes. Children below 3 are not permitted. Sexual activities, alcohol and smoking items are prohibited. Advance reservation is required at ashram-run guesthouses. At the ashram, passes are required by guests for various activities.

description: Dharbaneeswarar Temple (or Dharbaranyeswarar Temple), also known as Saniswaram Temple is located in the town of Thirunallar and is of great reverence among the followers of Lord Shiva and Lord Sani (Saturn). There are shrines of Lord Shiva (worshipped as Dharbaranyeswarar), Goddess Boghamaarthapoonmulaiyaal and Lord Sani within the temple complex. Saturday being the day of worshipping Sani attracts huge flocks of devotees to the temple. It is believed that the temple gets its name from the forest of Dharba (a type of grass) surrounding it. According to the popular mythological legend, King Nala got relieved from the influence of Sani by taking a bath at the holy tank at this place. Visitors to the temple take an oil bath at the tank and then pray to seek the blessings of Lord Sani. Prefer to visit on a Saturday if you are visiting the temple as a pilgrim. The temple stays open from 06:00 a.m.-08:00 p.m. on this day.

description: Named after Guru Gobind Singh, the 10th Sikh Guru, and constructed by Maharaja Ranjit Singh, better known as the Lion of Punjab, Gobindgarh Fort is the pride of the holy city of Amritsar. One of the most majestic structures of Punjab’s golden history, the fort came into existence in 1760 and was initially named as Bhangian da Qila. Annexed by the British in 1849 and handed over to the Indian Army in 1948, the fort was later handed over to the Punjab Government and has only recently been opened for tourism. A brick and lime structure with powerful ramparts and huge bastions, the fort boasts of two strong gates, the more popular one being the Nalwa Gate. The British made numerous additions to the fort, the most noteworthy ones being Hawa Mahal, Durbar Hall and Phansi Ghar (the execution place) where the revolutionaries of Indian freedom struggle were hanged. The local authorities are currently making great additions to the fort for attracting more number of tourists. A light and sound show and special lighting effects are expected soon.

description: One of the latest additions to India’s marvellous architectural wealth is the Golden Temple of Sripuram located near the village of Thirumalaikodi. Inaugurated in 2007, the temple is adorned with tonnes of pure gold foil embedded on copper base. Situated in a holy pond (containing water brought from all sacred rivers of India), the temple is accessible by walking on a 1.8 km. star-shaped path which represents Sri Chakra. The temple is dedicated to Goddess Narayani, a form of Goddess Mahalakshmi, and is headed by a self-styled godman Narayani Amma. The whole path leading to the temple has messages from the holy books Gita, Bible and Quran. The granite idol of the goddess, embellished with gold ornaments, is spellbinding and is believed to offer immense pleasure to the eyes. Spread over 100 acre, the whole complex is surrounded by the lush green beauty of small hills that add to the magnificence of the place. Though, there are no charges to visit and pray at the temple, the tourists facing time shortage can avail special passes (costing up to INR 250 per head) for the same. The temple glitter gold throughout the day and is definitely a wonder creation.

description: Gurudwara Ramsar Sahib was constructed in the year 1855 by Guru Arjun Dev, the fifth Guru of the Sikh religion. It is believed that to make the surroundings more serene, Guru ji dug a small holy tank (sarovar) and named it Ramsar Sahib after the name of his father Guru Ramdas. This place is considered to be one of the most sacred places since it is believed that Guruji along with his scribe have spent one year at this place and shared the principles of Sikhism by compiling the Adi Granth. This granth, is commonly known as the Guru Granth Sahib, the holy book of Sikh religion which embodies the scriptures and philosophy of this religion. In this complex, there is an eight storied building which is the printing press of the Guru Granth Sahib. A total of 40,000 volumes are printed here every year.

description: Described as the most unfortunate and shameful incident in the history of India’s struggle for freedom, Jallianwala Bagh Massacre in 1919 is deeply etched in the memory of every Indian. On the occasion of Baisakhi, thousands of people performing peaceful celebrations at the bagh (garden) were mercilessly subjected to about 1,600 rounds of bullets by the British troops. While hundreds of the innocent people died in the firing, many jumped into the well present in the garden in an attempt to save their live. The Indian Government established a memorial at the garden in 1951 which is a must-visit site on any visit to Amritsar. One can still find walls with uncountable bullet marks as well as the aforementioned well. The memorial in the shape of a flame honours the martyrs of the incident. An eternal flame which burns day and night was also added later. Other highlights of the place include a gallery displaying portraits of brave Indian freedom struggle revolutionaries and leaders and a light and sound show held every evening.

description: Dedicated to a memorable visit by Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth Sikh Guru, Gurudwara Charan Kanwal is located in the Machhiwara Town near the city of Ludhiana. According to the popular legend, the Guru, after escaping from the clutches of Mughal forces, quenched his thirst by drinking water from a well at this place and spent a full night sleeping under a jand tree. The tree is still present near the Sikh temple and the divine well is used to supply water to the gurudwara. A beautiful white structure surrounded by immense calmness, the gurudwara is visited by devotees from all religions. It is also believed that the Guru sang his popular hymn ‘Mitar Pyare Nu’ at the same place. Also, three of his popular five devotees (known as Panj Pyare) met him here. Every year, a huge fair is held at the holy place on the occasion of Baisakhi in the month of April.

monumentName: National Institute of Sports & Art Gallery
description: Popularly known as NIS, the National Institute of Sports & Art Gallery is the Asia’s biggest sports institute. This spectacular building houses a unique collection of sport memorabilia like PT Usha 1986 Seoul Asiad Shoes, the Adidas Spike worn by Milkha Singh, Major Dhyan Chand’s gold medal, a 95 kg hass used for squats by the Great Gama, a bat autographed by the Indian Cricket Team after winning 1983 World Cup. Informally renowned as the “Mecca” of Indian Sports, NIS works as the academic wing of the Sports Authority of India. Visitors can also find the replica of prestigious national sports award like the Arjuna Award and the Padam Shri Award. Renamed as Netaji Subhas National Institute of Sports, NIS and art gallery is located in the Old Moti Bagh Palace. Visitors can also explore other attractions in Patiala like Chattbir Zoo, Qila Mubarak, Sheesh Mahal and Moti Bagh Palace.

description: Also known as Dargah Sharif, this holy shrine is of great reverence among the Muslims and is visited by the devotees from all faiths. Located at the footsteps of the rustic Aravalli Hills, it is home to the Tomb of Sufi Saint Khawaja Moinuddin Chishti, who is believed to be the God’s messenger and fulfils the wishes of one and all. The huge complex around the shrine accommodates many historical buildings, monuments and mosques. Two huge degs (cauldrons) that are used for cooking pudding are among the key highlights. There are numerous shops at the bazaar from where one can purchase offerings in the form of chaddars (holy sheets) and flowers. The annual Urs Festival hosts great celebrations at this holy place. The city of Ajmer is home to many other popular attractions like Akbar’s Palace and Museum, Taragarh Fort, Ana Sagar Lake, Foy Sagar Lake, Adhai Din ka Jhonpda and Nareli Jain Temple. Warnings: Beware of beggars and be alert against pick pocketing. Men should cover their head and women should cover their head and shoulders.

description: Dargah Sharif, also known as Ajmer Sharif, is one of the most revered Muslim pilgrimage sites in the world. It is home to the holy mausoleum, where the Sufi saint Moinuddin Chishti rests in peace. The most striking aspect of the place is that followers of all religions driven by their immense faith visit the shrine. It is strongly believed that visiting the Dargah can purify one’s soul and connects directly to the Almighty. Colourful sheets (called chadars) are what the devout pilgrims offer at Dargah Sharif. Urs (death anniversary celebrations) is the best time to visit the place and experience the enthusiasm and devotion of pilgrims visiting in huge flocks. Kalanders (fondly called the friends of the saint) cover hundreds of miles on foot to reach the place every year. The premise has many structures boasting of splendid Mughal art and architecture worth exploring. Be sure to dress up properly while visiting the religious place.

description: One of the holiest Jain pilgrimage sites, Sri Mahavirji Temple is a white marble and red sandstone wonder, well-known for its sacred idol of Lord Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara in Jainism. According to the legend, the idol was miraculously found after excavating the hill where a cow would empty all her milk every single day. It is believed that a mere glance at the idol, made of sandstone in coral colour and depicting Mahavira in Padmasana, can eradicate all the sorrows. Carvings and paintings depicting the mythological scenes on the temple’s walls are extremely exquisite. Another attraction is the Manastambha (a beautiful column) which is about 52 ft. high and flaunts the idols of 4 Tirthankaras facing different directions. High pinnacles and attractive chattris (cenotaphs) are other remarkable features of the temple’s architecture. Located on the bank of River Gambhir, the temple complex also accommodates other small shrines which include Shantinath Jinalaya Temple, Kirti Ashram Chaityalaya Temple, Bhagwan Parshvanath Jinalaya Temple (known for its decorative glass work) and Bhavya Kamal Temple. The temple hosts a grand religious festival during the months of March and April every year.

monumentName: Keoladeo Ghana Bird Sanctuary and National Park
description: Earlier a royal waterfowl hunting ground, Keoladeo Ghana Bird Sanctuary and National Park today enjoys numerous internationally acclaimed designations. Named after a Shiva Temple, it is a protected bird sanctuary (since 1976), a national park (since 1982) and a World Heritage Site (since 1985). More than anything else, it is often tagged as the best bird-watching area in the whole world, welcoming hundreds of thousands of tourists, including ornithologists, from all over the world. The credit goes to the sanctuary’s ability to accommodate more than 360 species of birds, both indigenous and migratory. Storks, herons, cormorants, shoveler, white spoonbill, oriental ibis, darter, sarus crane, sandpiper and many other waterfowls in huge groups can be explored here. In addition, there are amazing species of land birds at the park, including warblers, partridges, babblers, chats, hornbills, quails and some of the most magnificent species of eagles. The park also features wild animals like blue bull, porcupine, spotted deer, wild boar, mongoose and jackals among others. Flora in the form of grasslands, woodlands, scrublands and aquatic vegetation bless the park with additional charm.

description: Bijolia (also known as Beejoliya Kalan) is a town known for its rich heritage and sacred temples belonging to Hinduism and Jainism. Located on a rugged plateau, it is a walled town with two gates in the north and south directions. It once emerged as a popular centre of art and architecture and one can easily follow the footprints of the same while in the town. Bijolia is famous for its fort which though in the state of ruins is still a noteworthy construction. The famous Shiva temple called Hajaresvara Mahadeva is located within the fort and is known for highlights carved archways, holy pond and more than hundred Shiva Lingams. Another attraction of the place is the series of five Jain Temples known for their rock inscriptions. The most revered of these temples is the one dedicated to Lord Parsvanath. The adjoining Teerth Kshetra where Parsvanath is believed to have performed penance hundreds of years ago is visited by thousands of pilgrims. Interesting petrographs can also be found nearby.

description: Since 1937, the Ganga Golden Jubilee Museum in Bikaner has been mesmerizing the visitors with rich collections of artefacts in numerous categories. Among the most distinguished exhibits are the terracotta masterpieces dating back to the Golden Gupta Period. Artefacts from the Indus Valley and Harappan excavation sites are the earliest specimen of the human evolution. Another amazing collection is that of lacquer work done on wood, stone, glass, leather, metal and surfaces as interesting as ostrich eggshells. Jain marble sculptures and bronze images discovered form the Rajasthan villages have spellbinding appeal. Equally charming are the artistic carvings done on wood and stone. When in Bikaner, you are never far from some splendid painting work and the miniature paintings from various regions of Rajasthan at the museum confirm this fact. Also displayed are the coins, royal farmaans (messages written on rolled fabrics), royal silk robes and jade and diamond studded royal weapons. Mesmerizing carpets and gilded leaf work on camel hide are simply outstanding. Stuffed lions and tigers are reminiscent of the courage that royal hunters exhibited. You can also explore ancient Sanskrit manuscripts, old photographs and Litho prints dating back to the British Era.
